annotations.label: Probability density function

anchor.snippet: a probability density function ( PDF ), density function , or simply density

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.pdf` and the `HasPDF` class define the probability density function of a random variable.

annotations.label: Bacteria lifespan example

anchor.snippet: Suppose bacteria of a certain species typically live 20 to 30 hours.

annotations.note: This is an informal pedagogical example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Density of an absolutely continuous random variable

anchor.snippet: non-negative Lebesgue-integrable function

annotations.note: `HasPDF` with `Real.hasPDF_iff` and `map_eq_setLIntegral_pdf` capture the univariate absolutely-continuous density `ℙ(X∈S)=∫_S f` for a general base measure.

anchors.snippet: non-negative Lebesgue-integrable function

annotations.label: Relation between density and CDF

anchor.snippet: is the cumulative distribution function of

annotations.note: The integral relation `ℙ(X∈S)=∫_S pdf` is `map_eq_setLIntegral_pdf` and `cdf` exists, but the differentiation link `f = F'` between pdf and cdf is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: is the cumulative distribution function of

annotations.label: Measure-theoretic density (Radon–Nikodym derivative)

anchor.snippet: is the Radon–Nikodym derivative

annotations.note: `pdf X ℙ μ` is defined exactly as `(map X ℙ).rnDeriv μ`, the Radon–Nikodym derivative of the law.

anchors.snippet: is the Radon–Nikodym derivative

annotations.label: PMF as density with respect to counting measure

anchor.snippet: is the density with respect to the counting measure over the sample space

annotations.note: `PMF.toMeasure` and the counting measure both exist, but there is no lemma stating a PMF equals the density of its measure with respect to `Measure.count`.

annotations.label: Almost-everywhere uniqueness of density

anchor.snippet: the density is almost unique, meaning that any two such densities coincide almost everywhere

annotations.note: `withDensity_eq_iff_of_sigmaFinite` shows two densities give the same measure iff they agree almost everywhere.

annotations.label: Uniform distribution with density greater than one

anchor.snippet: the continuous uniform distribution on the interval [0, 1/2] has probability density

annotations.note: `IsUniform.pdf_eq` gives the uniform density as `(μ s)⁻¹` on `s`, which equals 2 on `[0,1/2]`, illustrating densities exceeding one.

annotations.label: Standard normal density

anchor.snippet: The standard normal distribution has probability density

annotations.note: `gaussianPDFReal 0 1` is exactly the standard normal density `(1/√(2π))e^{-x²/2}`, a special case of the general Gaussian density.

anchors.snippet: The standard normal distribution has probability density

annotations.note: `pdf.integral_mul_eq_integral` proves `𝔼[X] = ∫ x·f(x) dx` for a real random variable with pdf `f`.

annotations.label: Not every distribution has a density

anchor.snippet: Not every probability distribution has a density function

annotations.note: `HasPDF` is a conditional class characterized by `hasPDF_iff` via absolute continuity, but there is no theorem exhibiting a distribution lacking a density.

annotations.label: Existence of density via absolutely continuous CDF

anchor.snippet: A distribution has a density function if its cumulative distribution function F ( x ) is absolutely continuous

annotations.note: `Real.hasPDF_iff` shows a density exists iff the law is absolutely continuous w.r.t. Lebesgue, but the link from CDF absolute continuity specifically is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: A distribution has a density function if its cumulative distribution function F ( x ) is absolutely continuous

annotations.label: One-point sets have zero probability

anchor.snippet: then the probability of every one-point set { a } is zero

annotations.note: `noAtoms_withDensity` shows any measure with a density w.r.t. an atomless measure (like Lebesgue) assigns measure zero to singletons.

annotations.label: Densities equal up to a measure-zero set

anchor.snippet: Two probability densities f and g represent the same probability distribution precisely if they differ only on a set of Lebesgue measure zero

annotations.note: `withDensity_eq_iff_of_sigmaFinite` states `μ.withDensity f = μ.withDensity g ↔ f =ᵐ[μ] g`, exactly the differ-on-a-null-set criterion.

anchor.section: Link between discrete and continuous distributions
anchor.snippet: a generalized probability density function using the Dirac delta function

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Measure.dirac` but no notion of a generalized (distributional) density built from Dirac deltas.

annotations.label: Parametrized families of densities

anchor.snippet: to be parametrized—that is, to be characterized by unspecified parameters

annotations.note: Specific parametrized densities (e.g. `gaussianPDFReal μ v`) exist, but there is no general formalization of a parametrized family of densities.

anchors.snippet: to be parametrized—that is, to be characterized by unspecified parameters

annotations.label: Joint probability density function

anchor.snippet: often called joint probability density function

annotations.note: `pdf` applies to a vector-valued variable `fun ω ↦ (X ω, Y ω)` with respect to `μ.prod ν`, giving the joint density used in `indepFun_iff_pdf_prod_eq_pdf_mul_pdf`.

anchors.snippet: often called joint probability density function

annotations.label: Joint PDF from CDF

anchor.snippet: the joint probability density function can be computed as a partial derivative

annotations.note: Mathlib's `cdf` is only defined on ℝ; there is no multivariate joint CDF nor its mixed partial derivative.

anchors.snippet: the joint probability density function can be computed as a partial derivative

annotations.label: Marginal density function

anchor.snippet: This is called the marginal density function

annotations.note: Projections of a joint-density variable are shown to have a pdf via `quasiMeasurePreserving_hasPDF'`, but the explicit marginal formula `f_X = ∫ f(x,y) dy` is not stated.

anchors.snippet: This is called the marginal density function

annotations.label: Independence via joint density

anchor.snippet: admitting a joint density are all independent from each other if

annotations.note: `indepFun_iff_pdf_prod_eq_pdf_mul_pdf` proves two variables are independent iff their joint density equals the product of the marginal densities.

anchors.snippet: admitting a joint density are all independent from each other if

annotations.label: Independence from factored joint density

anchor.snippet: can be factored into a product of n functions of one variable

annotations.note: The two-variable iff is formalized, but the corollary for an arbitrary factorization into one-variable functions and the n-variable version are not.

annotations.note: `pdf.integral_pdf_smul` is exactly the law of the unconscious statistician `𝔼[g(X)] = ∫ pdf X x • g x dx`.

annotations.label: Density of a sum is the convolution

anchor.snippet: is the convolution of their separate density functions

annotations.note: The `@[to_additive]` companion of `IndepFun.pdf_mul_eq_mlconvolution_pdf` proves the pdf of a sum of independent variables is the (additive) convolution of their densities.
